topic: HL Core Extension: Global Interactions (Power, Places & Networks; Human Development & Diversity; Global Risks & Resilience)
subject: geography

items.q: What is the difference between a natural hazard and a natural disaster?
items.correct: A natural hazard is a physical event with potential to cause harm; it becomes a disaster only when it affects vulnerable people and causes significant impact.
items.misconception: They are the same thing — any earthquake or hurricane is a disaster.
items.random_wrong: A hazard happens on land and a disaster happens at sea.
items.why: Earthquakes in unpopulated areas are hazards but not disasters. The 'disaster' status depends on human vulnerability — central to modern hazard geography.

items.q: What is the main criticism of Rostow's stages of economic growth?
items.correct: It assumes all countries can follow the same linear Western industrialisation path, ignoring colonial legacies, global trade inequalities and environmental constraints — a top-down modernisation view.
items.misconception: All LICs will pass through Rostow's five stages if they wait long enough.
items.random_wrong: Rostow's model is universally accepted as the best development theory.
items.why: Rostow assumed Western industrialisation as a template, but global economic structures may prevent LICs replicating it. Dependency theorists argue underdevelopment is actively produced — not just a delay.

items.q: How are fold mountains like the Himalayas formed?
items.correct: By the collision of two continental plates (India and Eurasia) where neither can subduct, so the crust crumples and is forced upwards over millions of years.
items.misconception: Fold mountains form when one plate slides under another into the mantle.
items.random_wrong: Fold mountains form from giant volcanoes erupting upwards.
items.why: Students confuse continent-continent collision with subduction. Continental crust is too buoyant to subduct, so it crumples — distinct from oceanic-continental boundaries like the Andes.
The answer examiners want is: Joseph Nye. “Robert Keohane” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Who proposed world systems theory in 1974?
items.why: It's Immanuel Wallerstein. “Andre Gunder Frank” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Approximately how much did India receive in remittances in 2022 ($ billions)?
items.why: The real figure is 111 billions USD. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
